item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one starting to notice a trend in Faminepulse's work? There's always something… about perspective and stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoy Series 3 articles that are short and sweet, and this definitely fits the bill. An interesting idea that is well-executed! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, I do, but I have an issue.

I know its been brought up before but personally I feel a Keter designation would be more fitting, simply because of its destructive potential. I mean, what happens if you send the thing to space? It looks at Earth, twitches once, boom, KX right then are there. I mean I understand the school of thought that "If you don't touch it then you'll be fine" but its potential as a weapon seems to simply massive for it to be Euclid. I dunno, just a thought.

And wait a minute what if it breaches containment and catches a glimpse of the sun?

Telekinetic Space Owl Destroyer…

Sounds like a nice band…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second the D-class's statement regarding the owl's cuteness. +1

item.title: for the record
item.content:encoded:
source of this image:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Northern_White-faced_Owl_in_2012.jpg

author's name is Nambon.

photo is licensed as Creative Commons-Attribution 2.0, which means anyone can use it as long as they properly attribute the creator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, gave it another go, and I like it! Here are some issues:

+ show block
– hide block

(Sorry I don't have text copied over, I'm on my phone.)
- It's a bit odd that your containment starts in present tense description rather than in the form of instructions.
- Why blindfolded rather than hooded? Just odd when there's already a technical term/tool (from falconry) available.
- Transmogrify isn't a scientific term, making it pretty vague for containment. It does sound cool, just not very technical.
- "might suggest" is redundant.
- You mention that the shrunken things get smaller relative to the bird's perspective, but isn't the idea that from the bird's perspective, they're staying the same size?
- "likelihood…is more likely" is redundant/not well worded.

I did enjoy it overall. It's a pretty simple SCP, but it's a nice exploration of what a non-human type green does with their abilities. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it but I noticed a problem.

"SCP-2862 observing a nearby subject during its first days of containment."

I know this is kinda pushing it but why is the room white instead of black? You should put a reason why it's white. Maybe it was located there?

Ehh it's kinda a nitpick 1+ because it's good

EDIT : oh I see, first DAYS of containment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
saying that an owl doesn't understand perceptual phenomena is… well, wildly incorrect.

I meant that an owl doesn't have an intellectual understanding of perspective, or of optical illusions. Humans didn't until the Renaissance!

This owl was born with these abilities, it always could grab the small things and so on, so it never learned otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am going to do another read through of this to make sure everything as clear as possible…

I chose a bird because it was perfect for the concept: an anomalous animal that has learned behaviors related to its anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One big thing to consider is that birds seem to rely a lot on the parallax effect to judge distance and scale- that's why they bob and tilt their heads so much when examining something when sitting still, and do their best to lock their heads relative to their surroundings when flying, walking, or perched on something that moves. If their eyes are moving, something is moving.

By retreating to the corner of an otherwise empty room, the D-class eliminated all possible parallax that could indicate definite scale. Since the owl seems to be sapient enough to selectively apply its abilities, it's possible that it's sapient enough to intentionally let itself be fooled by optical illusions when it wants to be- and the d-class unintentionally set up a perfect one for a bird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it's not my specialty, but from the field of ecological psychology as described by Gibson, saying that an owl doesn't understand perceptual phenomena is… well, wildly incorrect.
Science ramble!

+ show block
– hide block

Basically, for vision, the bodies and perceptual systems of animals (including humans) evolved to gather information about the environment through 1) observing the reflected light in the environment which behaves in specific ways due to physics and 2) intentionally altering their perspective in order to identify elements that change (e.g. whole visual field moves during head movement) and those that do not (e.g. a car or mouse staying still in your visual field as you turn your head). This extends to things like parralax and interpolation associated with perspective. Your perceptual-motor system automatically extracts this information in a predictable manner consistent with the laws of physics (lots of experimental evidence for this), so the idea of "needing to understand perspective" is unnecessary. Owls are way better than us at tracking movement and distance, so it's kind of ridiculous to say that we're better at it than them.

So says Gibson (And his subsequent disciples), anyway. Then you get into things like intrinsic scaling and affordances, which although neat, are literally at least 1.5 grad courses.

ALL THAT RAMBLING ASIDE! I didn't quite understand what was going on in the first read through, possibly due to fatigue. I'll give this another shot when I'm more cognizant. <3 transformer owls, though.

Edit: I also haven't thought about how eco psych applies to this SCP, but I'm not using that as criteria.
Edit edit: Check this thing out: owl like, damn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a great read. It's nice to see a reality bending skip that doesn't feel overpowered or nerfed in fear of criticism. I also just adore reality bending objects that distort things based off of their perception of them.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My read:
It has space warping powers and doesn't understand perspective or other perceptual phenomenon (because it is an owl). So if you are far away it sees you as small but close and can grab and eat you; if it turns its head the image rotates, so you rotate; if it squints, the image compresses and you compress.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't entirely get this, but from the looks of it the owl has some kind of telekinesis that depends on its perception?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Accidentally reverted an edit I did in the 2nd test whoops and realized I messed up anyway, so I'm just gonna make a post for it instead of possibly measing it up further.

D-0551 moves to touch SCP-2862. SCP-2862 screeches and contracts its body.
SCP-2862 squints.

In the page source (which is how I usually read pages, for reasons) there's a ">" between the main part of the quote and "SCP-2862 squints", with no spacing, making it useless.
The part I messed up btw, was I forgot to itialicize it when I added the space.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been a long time since I saw an article this short that made me interested. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2862 moves to touch SCP-2862. SCP-2862
screeches and contracts its body.

Is this intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I loved it. It took me a second to get what it did, but once I got it, man was I horrified. Amazing work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cracks knuckles.

Alright. Its review time. Overall I liked it. It uses the owl's predatory abilities and ability to rotate its head to creepy effects, and I enjoy the simplicity of the article.

Some minor nitpicks:

SCP-2862 is contained in an empty and featureless 15 sq m room.

I wonder if this would be better expressed as 15 m2.

In the case of containment breach

Maybe try "In the event of a containment breach.

Also, I have to wonder what is preventing scaled down individuals from asphyxiating due tot heir reduced size. That might be a neat direction to expand the article, but if you choose not to, its not a deal breaker.

+1. This article was a hoot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so I've finally plucked up the courage to write my first review (baby steps ftw): This SCP has been the first in a while to make me say to my self "Dude, that's not right…"
And that's a good thing. My spine tingled sideways while I was trying to grasp the concept of what it actually is that this SCP does. Afterwards, my head hurt.

One small thing though, I thought the ozone thing didn't really add anything to the narrative and may as well have been left out, but I'll admit that I may just be not seeing something that's practically screaming in my face.

All in all, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I think that's right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I like the idea, a small note is that for me it seems less Keter and more Euclid. I know the effects re bad once you look at it, but all you need to do is blindfold it. Anyhow, that's about it for now. Neutral vote. Lemme retract my -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Kola and Salvagebar for draft reviews.
– hide block
